content.text: The Anthropic Console includes a built-in evaluation tool that lets you run a prompt against a set of test cases, grade the outputs, and compare results across prompt versions — entirely in the browser, with no code required. It is the fastest way to validate a prompt change before committing to a code-based eval setup.

attrs.alt: Anthropic Console eval workflow and grader options

content.text: What the Console Eval Tool Does

The Console eval tool covers the core eval loop: write a prompt, supply test cases, run the model against all of them, and grade the outputs. It is not a replacement for a programmatic eval pipeline — it is a low-friction entry point that bridges the prompt-editing experience and the evaluation experience in one interface.
The four stages map to the same pipeline any eval runner implements:
Write or paste your system prompt in the Prompt Editor, using {{variable}} placeholders for input fields.
Import test cases from a CSV/JSON file or enter them manually. Each row maps to one placeholder in the prompt.
Click Run to send all cases to the model in parallel. Outputs appear inline as they stream in.
Select a grader, score the outputs, and compare scores across two prompt versions side by side.
Structuring Prompts with Variables

The Console uses double-brace syntax for variable substitution: any {{name}} in the system prompt or user turn is replaced by the corresponding column from your test case dataset. This is the same pattern used in most prompt templating libraries, so prompts written in the Console can be copied directly to code.

System prompt (in Console prompt editor):
You are a customer support agent for {{company_name}}.
Your tone should be {{tone}}.
Never discuss competitors or pricing.

User turn template:
{{customer_message}}
A matching CSV test case file has columns named company_name, tone, and customer_message. The Console fills in the values for each row when the run starts.

company_name,tone,customer_message
Acme Corp,formal,My order hasn't arrived after two weeks.
Acme Corp,friendly,Can I return a product I bought three months ago?
Acme Corp,formal,I was charged twice for the same order.
Keep variable names short and lowercase. The Console is case-sensitive: {{Company_Name}} and {{company_name}} are different slots.
Grading Options
The Console offers three grader types, each suited to a different task category:
Exact Match

The output must equal the expected string exactly (or with case folding, depending on the toggle). Correct for tasks with deterministic answers: classification labels, yes/no responses, structured JSON keys.
Input
Expected
Output
Score
Is this a refund request? (yes/no)
yes
yes
Is this a refund request? (yes/no)
yes
Yes, it is.
0.0 — not exact
Category: billing, shipping, or other?
billing
billing
Exact match fails as soon as the model adds any surrounding text. For tasks where the model naturally writes prose, use contains or LLM-as-judge instead.
Contains / Regex
The output passes if it contains a specified keyword or matches a regular expression pattern. More forgiving than exact match while still being deterministic and fast.
Contains: output includes the substring 'refund processed' — useful for confirming action words appear
Regex: output matches \d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} — confirms a date was formatted correctly
Regex: output does not match (?i)competitor_name — confirms the model avoided a forbidden term

Grader type: regex
Pattern: ^(billing|shipping|technical|other)$
Flags: case-insensitive

Matches outputs like:
  "billing"     ✓
  "Billing"     ✓
  "BILLING"     ✓
  "I think billing."  ✗ (leading text fails anchored regex)
LLM-as-Judge

The Console sends each (input, output) pair to Claude with a grading rubric and returns a numeric score. This is the only grader capable of evaluating open-ended quality: helpfulness, tone appropriateness, factual accuracy relative to a reference, or adherence to complex instructions.
When you select LLM-as-judge, the Console offers a default rubric template and lets you customise it. The template looks like this:
The score is averaged across all cases to produce a run-level metric. The per-case reason strings are shown in the results table and are the primary debugging tool when a case scores unexpectedly.
Comparing Prompt Versions

The Console's comparison feature is its most distinctive capability relative to a plain API call. You can create two prompt variants (A and B) and run both against the same test cases in a single session. Results appear in a side-by-side table with scores highlighted when they differ.
Typical use cases for the comparison view:
Before/after a system prompt edit — confirm the change improves target cases without regressing others
Model version comparison — same prompt sent to two different Claude models
Instruction style comparison — detailed vs. concise instruction phrasing
Format variation — JSON output format instruction vs. natural language format instruction

The comparison view shows you which cases changed and in which direction. A case that improved from score 2 to score 5 and another that regressed from 5 to 3 in the same run is a trade-off worth investigating — not an overall win.
Importing and Exporting Test Cases
The Console accepts test case datasets as CSV or JSON. CSV is the most convenient format for small datasets:

# CSV format — one row per test case
input,expected,criteria
"Summarize this article in one sentence: [...]","<gold summary>","Factual, concise, no editorializing"
"What is the capital of France?","Paris","Exact factual answer"
JSON is more flexible and supports multi-line inputs without CSV escaping:

After a run, export results as CSV. The export includes the input, expected value, model output, score, and (for LLM judges) the reason string. Import this export into a spreadsheet for annotation or into your code-based eval suite as labeled cases.
Practical Workflow: Prompt Iteration with Console Evals
The Console eval tool fits naturally into a prompt-iteration loop before a programmatic eval suite exists:
Draft an initial system prompt in the Console editor.
Enter 10-20 representative test cases manually or import a small CSV.
Run with LLM-as-judge grader; note which cases score below 4/5.
Edit the prompt to address the low-scoring cases.
Re-run and compare the two versions side by side.
Once the suite grows past ~50 cases or you need CI integration, export cases and port the runner to code.

This workflow catches most prompt-level regressions before any code is written. It is especially useful when the prompt writer and the engineer are different people: the writer iterates in the Console while the engineer sets up the programmatic pipeline in parallel.
Limitations of the Console Eval Tool
Understanding where the Console tool stops being sufficient prevents it from becoming a bottleneck:
Limitation
Impact
When It Matters
No CI integration
Cannot block a deployment on a score regression
Any production system
No custom grader code
Graders limited to exact match, contains, regex, LLM judge
Execution-based grading, complex assertions
No suite versioning
No built-in hash or change tracking across sessions
Tracking trends over time
Scale ceiling (~200 cases)
Larger suites are slow and unwieldy in the UI
Stable production eval suites
No sub-group analysis
Cannot filter results by tag or stratum in the UI
Diagnosing slice-level failures
Manual scheduling
Runs happen when you click, not on a schedule
Nightly or per-commit automation
None of these are design flaws — they reflect the Console tool's intended scope: fast, no-code prototype validation. When you need any of the capabilities in the Impact column, move to a script.
Exporting to a Code-Based Pipeline
The Console eval tool and a code-based runner are not alternatives — they are stages in the same pipeline. When you are ready to transition:
Export your test cases from the Console as CSV.
Write a conversion script to format them as JSONL with the fields your runner expects.
Re-implement the grader logic in Python (exact match, regex, or LLM judge call).
Run once to verify the code-based score matches the Console score on the same cases.
Add the runner to CI and retire the manual Console runs for that suite.

import csv, json, pathlib

def console_csv_to_jsonl(csv_path: str, jsonl_path: str) -> None:
    """Convert a Console eval export CSV to the JSONL format used by the custom runner."""
    cases = []
    with open(csv_path) as f:
        for row in csv.DictReader(f):
            cases.append({
                "id": row.get("id") or f"case-{len(cases)+1:04d}",
                "input": row["input"],
                "expected": row.get("expected", ""),
                "criteria": row.get("criteria", ""),
                "grader": "llm_judge" if row.get("criteria") else "contains",
            })
    pathlib.Path(jsonl_path).write_text("\n".join(json.dumps(c) for c in cases))
    print(f"Converted {len(cases)} cases to {jsonl_path}")

# console_csv_to_jsonl("console_export.csv", "suites/core.jsonl")
Interpreting Per-Case Results
The results table in the Console shows one row per test case with the input, expected value, model output, and score. Reading this table effectively accelerates debugging:
Low-scoring cases cluster by pattern.

Sort by score ascending and look at the first five rows. Common patterns — all long inputs, all cases about a specific topic, all cases where the user phrase includes negation — reveal systematic weaknesses rather than random noise.
High variance across similar inputs signals prompt sensitivity.
If two inputs differ by only one word yet produce scores of 5 and 1 respectively, the prompt is underspecified and the model is latching onto a superficial feature.
The reason string is the primary signal for LLM judges.
A score of 2 with reason 'Response was factually correct but failed to address the emotional tone of the query' tells you exactly what to fix in the prompt.
Do not average the scores and move on. The aggregate score is a progress indicator, not an explanation. The per-case table is where the actual information lives.
Calibrating the LLM Judge in the Console
The Console's LLM judge can drift if the rubric is under-specified. Two calibration steps improve reliability before you commit to a rubric:
Anchor with Known Examples

Before running the full suite, manually score 5-10 cases yourself — note whether each should be a 1, 3, or 5. Then run the LLM judge on those same cases and compare. If the judge scores a case you rated as 5 as a 2, examine the reason string and adjust the rubric until the judge's top and bottom cases match your intuition.
Avoid Rubric Ambiguity
Rubric criteria with overlapping levels cause inconsistent judge behaviour. Compare these two rubric definitions for a summarisation task:
Version
Level 3 Definition
Level 4 Definition
Problem
Ambiguous
Adequate summary with some issues
Good summary, mostly complete
No clear distinction between 3 and 4
Clear
Covers main point; omits at least one key detail
Covers all key details; minor phrasing issues only
Each level has an observable criterion
The clear version produces consistent scores because each level has a falsifiable condition. The ambiguous version leaves the judge to fill in the distinction, which introduces variance.
Using Console Evals for Prompt Regression Testing
The Console eval tool can function as a lightweight regression suite even without CI integration, as long as you follow a consistent protocol:
After every significant prompt change, run the full current suite.
Export results to CSV with a filename that includes the date and model version.
Compare the aggregate score and any case-level regressions to the previous CSV.
If a case that scored 5 now scores 1, trace the regression to the specific prompt change before shipping.

This manual process is slower than automated CI but catches regressions that visual prompt inspection misses. It also creates an audit trail: a folder of dated CSV exports is a simple version history of your eval results.
Multi-Turn Evaluation in the Console

The Console prompt editor supports multi-turn conversations. You can add user turns and assistant turns to simulate a dialogue before the evaluated response. This is useful for testing how the model maintains context or handles follow-up corrections.

System:
You are a concise assistant. Answer in one sentence.

User:
What is the boiling point of water at sea level?

Assistant:
Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit) at sea level.

User (evaluated turn):
What about at high altitude?

The grader scores only the final assistant response — the preceding turns are context. Multi-turn eval cases are harder to create but expose failures that single-turn cases hide, such as ignoring prior context, contradicting an earlier answer, or failing to apply a correction the user provided.
Sharing Results with Stakeholders
The Console's CSV export is the primary sharing mechanism. For non-technical stakeholders reviewing eval quality, a formatted spreadsheet is more useful than raw CSV:
Rename column headers to plain English (e.g. 'model_output' to 'What the model said')
Add a colour scale to the score column (red for 1-2, yellow for 3, green for 4-5)
Filter to show only cases that scored below your acceptance threshold
Include the judge reason string as a tooltip or adjacent column for each low-scoring case

Sharing formatted eval results with product managers or domain experts before a launch is a forcing function: it exposes failure modes that engineering review alone misses, and it surfaces cases where the model is technically correct but misaligned with what the business actually wants.
Summary

The Anthropic Console eval tool provides a no-code path from prompt drafting to scored evaluation. It is the right starting point for any new prompt — fast to set up, easy to share, and sufficient for suites under a few hundred cases.
Use {{variable}} placeholders in your prompt to bind test case columns
Choose exact match for deterministic outputs, regex for format checks, LLM judge for open-ended quality
Use the side-by-side comparison view to validate prompt edits before shipping
Export your test cases as CSV to seed a code-based pipeline when you outgrow the Console
Switch to a script when you need CI integration, custom graders, or suites larger than ~200 cases
